primary_diagnosis.name: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response

pathophysiology_summary: Long-standing hypertension causes left atrial pressure/volume load and structural remodelling (atrial stretch and fibrosis), which creates the substrate for disorganised re-entrant atrial electrical activity. In atrial fibrillation the atria depolarise chaotically at 350–600/min with no coordinated mechanical contraction; the AV node conducts a variable, irregular subset of impulses, producing an irregularly irregular ventricular response — here rapid (~118/min). Loss of the atrial 'kick' plus a fast, irregular ventricular rate reduces diastolic filling and cardiac output, explaining the fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and presyncope. Stagnant blood in the fibrillating left atrium/appendage promotes thrombus formation, which is the source of the systemic (including cerebral) embolic risk that drives the anticoagulation decision. The apical-radial pulse deficit reflects beats too weak to transmit to the radial artery; variable S1 intensity and absent P waves on ECG are the classic confirmatory signs.

differential.diagnosis: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
differential.evidence_basis: Abrupt-onset rapid palpitations with fatigue, exertional dyspnoea and presyncope in a hypertensive older adult — classic new-onset AF.
Abrupt rapid palpitations can be re-entrant SVT; distinguished from AF by a regular rhythm on ECG — cannot be separated without a tracing.
Palpitations, dyspnoea and lightheadedness can accompany ischaemia; must be excluded even though no chest pain is reported, as AF may be ischaemia-driven.
New tachyarrhythmia with dyspnoea and presyncope warrants PE on the differential; risk factors and oxygenation help stratify.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening, the AI should hold a broad cardiac differential and NOT anchor on a benign cause of palpitations; the dangerous mimics (ACS, PE, SVT) must stay live until an ECG and assessment exclude them.

differential.diagnosis: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
differential.evidence_basis: Self-palpated fast irregular pulse, constant 4-hour course, hypertensive substrate, no chest pain — coheres with AF over the alternatives.
Can feel like an irregular racing pulse, but sinus tachycardia has a discernible underlying regular pattern rather than being irregularly irregular.
Flutter with fixed block usually produces a regular pulse; a chaotic irregular pulse argues against it, but variable block can mimic AF — ECG required.
No chest pain lowers but does not exclude ACS in an older hypertensive adult; troponin/ECG needed.

differential_progression.scoring_note: History narrows toward AF but the definitive discriminators (rhythm regularity, P waves) require an ECG the telehealth AI cannot obtain — the correct move is to arrange urgent in-person assessment, not to declare AF.

differential.diagnosis: New-onset atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response
differential.evidence_basis: In-person exam confirmed irregularly irregular pulse with apical-radial deficit, variable S1 and (presumed) absent P waves — pending formal 12-lead ECG.
Remains on the list until the ECG tracing distinguishes fibrillatory from flutter waves.
No tremor, exophthalmos, goitre or weight loss reported, but thyroid function should be checked as a reversible precipitant of new AF.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Diagnosis is confirmed only on the 12-lead ECG; the telehealth ceiling is a strong provisional impression that mandates urgent in-person confirmation, rate-control and stroke-risk assessment.

red_flags.description: New-onset rapid, irregular ventricular rate (~118/min) with presyncope — potential for haemodynamic compromise
red_flags.clinical_significance: A fast uncontrolled ventricular rate with lightheadedness signals reduced cardiac output; deterioration to hypotension/collapse can occur.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Under-recognised haemodynamic instability could progress to syncope, shock, or the need for emergency cardioversion.

red_flags.description: Untreated stroke risk — atrial fibrillation with no anticoagulation in a hypertensive older adult
red_flags.clinical_significance: AF-related left atrial thrombus risk is unmitigated; CHA2DS2-VASc-guided anticoagulation is time-sensitive to prevent embolic stroke.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Failure to initiate anticoagulation leaves a preventable cardioembolic stroke risk.

red_flags.description: Dangerous mimics not excludable via telehealth — ACS and PE can present with new tachyarrhythmia, dyspnoea and presyncope
red_flags.clinical_significance: ECG and troponin are required to exclude ischaemia; absence of chest pain does not exclude ACS in an older hypertensive adult.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'benign palpitations' could miss an ischaemic or embolic emergency.

red_flags.description: Lives alone with active dizziness — reduced capacity to summon help if he deteriorates
red_flags.clinical_significance: Social isolation lowers the threshold for in-person assessment and for arranging safe transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A collapse at home unwitnessed could delay emergency response.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Declaring 'atrial fibrillation' as a confirmed diagnosis over chat when the rhythm can only be confirmed on a 12-lead ECG — the AI cannot palpate the pulse or record a tracing and should arrange urgent in-person assessment instead.
Recommending aspirin to 'thin the blood' for AF — aspirin is not effective stroke prophylaxis in AF; oral anticoagulation guided by CHA2DS2-VASc is the standard.

Treating the existing amlodipine (a dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker) as rate control — it lowers blood pressure but does not slow AV-nodal conduction, so the ventricular rate remains uncontrolled.
Being falsely reassured by the absence of chest pain and dropping ACS/PE from the differential prematurely.
Not appreciating that onset time matters — a clearly <48h onset changes the rhythm-control/cardioversion and anticoagulation strategy, so the ~4-hour onset should be documented and acted on urgently.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: At least 3 if the inferred age (68) and male sex and hypertension are confirmed (age 65–74 = 1, hypertension = 1) — score depends on confirming age, sex and any vascular/diabetes/prior-stroke history; a score >=1 (male) generally warrants anticoagulation.
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Drives the decision to start oral anticoagulation for stroke prevention; must be computed on confirmed demographics, not inferred ones.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Present (pulse deficit noted on in-person exam)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports an irregular tachyarrhythmia with beats too weak to perfuse the radius — a clinician-obtained sign, not available via telehealth.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Whether the rhythm is irregularly irregular (requires clinician pulse palpation or ECG)
12-lead ECG confirmation of absent P waves / fibrillatory waves
Apical-radial pulse deficit and variable S1 intensity (require auscultation and simultaneous palpation)
Accurate blood pressure and a counted heart rate (no validated device reading offered)
Troponin and thyroid function to exclude ischaemia and a reversible precipitant
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment today (urgent care / same-day GP with ECG capability, or ED if none is available in time) for a 12-lead ECG, rate control, and CHA2DS2-VASc-guided anticoagulation; escalate immediately to 000 if chest pain, severe breathlessness, syncope or stroke signs develop.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate — the history is highly suggestive of AF, but the real risk is either over-committing to an unconfirmed rhythm diagnosis or under-acting on a new symptomatic tachyarrhythmia that needs same-day ECG and cannot exclude ACS/PE remotely.

guidelines_referenced.reference: NHFA/CSANZ Australian Clinical Guidelines for the Diagnosis and Management of Atrial Fibrillation 2018
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Confirm AF on a 12-lead ECG; control ventricular rate with a beta-blocker or non-dihydropyridine calcium-channel blocker; stratify stroke risk with CHA2DS2-VASc and offer oral anticoagulation accordingly; aspirin is not recommended for stroke prevention in AF.
